questionText: A curve has parametric equations $x = t^{2}$ and $y = 2t$. Find the coordinates of the points where this curve meets the straight line $y = x - 3$.

steps.description: Substitute the parametric coordinates into the line
steps.explanation: A point lies on both the curve and the line when its coordinates satisfy the line equation. Replacing $x$ and $y$ by their parametric expressions turns the line equation into a single equation in $t$.

steps.description: Rearrange into a quadratic in t
steps.explanation: Collecting every term on one side gives a quadratic in $t$, whose solutions are exactly the parameter values at the crossing points.
We look for two numbers that multiply to $-3$ and add to $-2$, namely $-3$ and $+1$. Factorising is the quickest route to the roots here.
Setting each bracket to zero gives the two parameter values. Both are real, so the line genuinely cuts the curve twice.
There is no restriction on $t$ for this curve, so neither value is rejected; each corresponds to a genuine point on the curve.
Substituting $t = 3$ back into the parametric equations gives the actual point on the curve.
Doing the same with $t = -1$ produces the second crossing point.

steps.description: Verify both points lie on the line
steps.explanation: A quick check that each point satisfies $y = x - 3$ confirms the algebra is correct.
These are the two points where the curve and line meet.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The curve meets the line at $(9,\,6)$ (when $t = 3$) and $(1,\,-2)$ (when $t = -1$).
